chapters.title: Ukraine combines the influence of the East and the West but has its unique identity
chapters.text:
The terrible events of recent years in Ukraine have drawn the world’s attention. Due to the lack of awareness before 2022, many people didn’t even know where Ukraine was. Putin saw it as an extension of Russia, and the West perceived Ukraine as another Central European state, a kind of “proto-Poland.” Nevertheless, many factors contributed to Ukraine’s in-between identity; it was neither the East’s nor the West’s perception. Ukrainian sociologists called the “Nestor group” investigated the phenomenon. They declared that, based on language and bureaucracy, neither the Russian model nor the Central European model is suitable for the Ukrainian people. The nation’s attitude toward family, church, and small business sways Ukraine more to the Mediterranean cultures of Italy or Greece.

Ukraine is about polyphony; its land is at the intersection of different cultures and breaks all stereotypes. Here, the citizens’ bilingualism contradicts Johann Herder's thesis that language defines a nation, Muslims and Jews unite for mutual aid, and nationalism exists alongside democracy. Ukrainian history is non-linear and complex, but this chaotic vortex creates notable figures.

The Ukrainian does not live thanks to history, with history, or in history, but near history and most often against it. ~ Andrii Bondar

Volodymyr Yermolenko

Ukraine remains terra incognita even for its people, but Ukrainians feel their otherness subconsciously and try to grasp their complex identity. Foreigners often perceive Ukraine through stereotypes — fat, hopak, corruption, the Klitschko brothers, Maidan, and war. At the same time, avant-garde artist Kazimir Malevich, neo-romantic poet Mykola Khvylovy, and the philosopher ahead of his time, Hryhoriy Skovoroda, remain overlooked. Ukraine isn’t a mere agricultural state focusing on rural traditional culture. It is also a modern society with electronic government services, world-famous IT companies, fashion brands, and creative advertising campaigns. Alongside corruption and the oligarchy, talented youth and literary and musical figures glorify the name of the Motherland abroad.

Ukraine tempts and perplexes because parts of its complex identity exist simultaneously and form a unique meaning.

The essays in this summary will reveal the ruins of empires, broken destinies, and extraordinary people who seek a happy life in peace on their land while facing adversity with positive thinking.

chapters.title: Agricultural land takes up 54% of Ukraine
chapters.text:
Black soil has always been crucial in Ukraine’s economic development and has repeatedly influenced its history. For example, fertile lands ensured conditions for agriculture so the local tribes could lead a sedentary lifestyle. Trypil culture became such a civilization. Herodotus was the first to mention it in his History, where he writes about the “Scythian plowmen” who lived north of the Black Sea steppe. History witnessed many confrontations between warlike nomads and agricultural tribes — the first usually the victors due to their specific lifestyle. In the 8th-13th centuries, nomads from the north, the so-called Vikings, gained control over the lands stretching along the path “from the Varangians to the Greeks.” They established their empire of Rus with the center in Kyiv, headed by Rurik (Eric), son Igor (Ingvar), and his wife Olga (Helga).

Arab philosopher Ibn-Khaldun described the cyclical attacks theory of nomadic tribes. After their conquest, nomads assimilate and adopt a particular lifestyle. Then, new nomadic tribes come, and the cycle repeats. The same thing happened to Kyivan Rus during the feudal fragmentation following Yaroslav’s death. In 1240, tribes from Mongolia attacked these lands. An exciting feature of statehood was the elite’s vast material wealth with a low level of culture.

Subsequently, from the 14th to the 18th century, Polish authorities gained control over the Ukrainian lands, the Ukrainian language spread, and a national identity began to form. Landlords forced peasants into serfdom to reduce the cost of bread production. Ukrainian serfs were the equivalent of enslaved people working the plantations in America.

At this time, those who escaped from serfdom often became Cossacks. They engaged in farming and robbery, reminiscent of the nomadic peoples, but the difference was the practice of Christianity. Kyivan Rus became Ukraine, and after the uprising led by Bohdan Khmelnytsky, Muscovy (modern Russia) took a part of its lands. When Muscovy conquered the Crimean Khanate, Ukrainian lands gained access to the sea; therefore, they could sell grain worldwide.

The Ukrainian culture of the modern period was mainly rural due to the loss of the elite during the Polish and Russian rule.

chapters.title: The Cossacks — an analog of the Vikings
chapters.text:
“Ukraine” as a self-title appeared in the continuation of “The Tale of Medieval Years” in the Kyiv Chronicle. This term means “border area,” which includes the border and its adjacent lands on both sides. Interestingly, the Cossacks arose directly on the steppe border, so there would be no Ukraine without them. They created a state called the Zaporizhzhia Army and then Hetmanship and Ukraine. The word “Cossack” is of Turkic origin and has many meanings, including “warrior,” “guard,” and “robber.”

The first Cossacks were Tatars, but unlike the Zaporizhizhians, they were nomads. Zaporizhzhian Cossacks were similar to musketeers. They had few horses to fight, but they had muskets in the 17th century, which was the prototype of the Kalashnikov assault rifle of the 20th century. The Cossacks were a heterogeneous social class that included rebels, warriors, robbers, and merchants.

Since the appearance of the Cossack hetmans, the names of the lands changed, and the princes’ ranks began to vanish. The uncrowned king of Russia, Konstantin Ostrozkyi, is a vivid example of the princely tradition. After his rule, the hetmans became the ruling elite. In the 18th century, a Cossack aristocracy emerged. Apart from a military unit, they also assumed administrative power.

Ukraine, Russia, and Belarus have roughly equal heritage of Kyivan Rus. However, Moscow often appeals to this thesis to justify its attempts to invade Kyiv.

The freedom-loving warrior image inspired many cultural figures, the most famous being Ukrainian poet and writer Taras Shevchenko. He actively popularized the Cossack myth in his works and saw the “History of the Rus” as the real history of Ukraine.

So, the question arises: Were the Cossacks a nation or the elite? Narodnik movement representative Mykhailo Hrushevskyi was critical of the Cossacks. Narodniks believed that the masses should play a central role in the state-building process and disregarded the aristocracy as part of the nation. Coming from the Polish elite, Vyacheslav Lipynskyi criticized this populist movement. Later, the elites began recapturing territories under Pavel Skoropadskyi’s leadership.

In the 1920s, the era of fascism, Nazism, and radical nationalism prevailed in Europe. Meanwhile, Dmytro Dontsov and Yevhen Malaniuk offer an elitist approach to Ukrainian identity. The new generation led by Stepan Bandera and Lev Rebet reflects a new ideology and a complete departure from populism.

chapters.title: Ukrainians and Russians differ on the cultural and mental levels
chapters.text:
Although the concept of “Soviet culture” was artificial, it firmly occupied many people’s minds. It lives to this day, despite the collapse of the Soviet Union, especially in the adult generation that grew up in the 20th century and many foreigners. However, the Soviet Union and Ukraine are opposites: the former is the old name for the Russian Federation, which has not changed since, while Ukraine is a separate state. For Russians with imperialist views, Belarus and Ukraine are territories they have lost control over temporarily.

One of the critical differences between Ukrainians and Russians is in the surnames: Russian ones end with -ov (Ivanov, Sidorov) and -ev (Sazhantsev), and Ukrainian ones have -ko (Shevchenko) and -uk (Bondarchuk). Inherent character traits also differ according to both sides’ perceptions. For example, many Russians believe Ukrainians are greedy and cunning, while Russians are simple and open. On the other hand, in the Ukrainian imagination, Russians are careless drunkards, and Ukrainians are hardworking and skillful. Differences also lie in traditions. There is a saying in Russia: “What's a wedding without a good fight?” YouTube has much video evidence. In Ukraine, it is customary to end the feast with loud choral singing.

The image of the “Soviet man” that Vladimir Lenin and Joseph Stalin created and sought to instill intended to diminish ethnic groups’ diversity. Both leaders saw this process as problematic in Ukraine because of the growing resistance of the Ukrainian national character.

Ukraine has become a nation of independent individualists under Soviet rule.

Ukrainian and Russian political systems also vary. Russia has always been a monarchy, so its people are used to the personality cult of the tsar, evident in Soviet ideology. Russians united to kill one ruler and love the next. Ukraine wasn’t a monarchy — it immediately jumped from the princely feudal system to the hetman-Cossack system. Moreover, at the turn of the 16th and 17th centuries, a matrix of democratic anarchy emerged in society. Similarly, modern Ukrainians actively participate in the state’s political life: they vote, support their candidates, and organize revolutions when the leader doesn’t act in the nation’s interests. Since the hetman’s election, Ukrainians have been electing their representatives, while Russians believed God chose the king.

In 2014, Russia violated the territorial integrity of Ukraine, explaining it as protecting the Russian-speaking population in the Donetsk and Luhansk regions. The Russian Empire has used Russification to seize new lands since ancient times. In the Soviet Union, they used communist ideology and sent officials from the Far East and Siberia to complete the task. However, many Ukrainian people retained their opinion. After the declaration of independence in 1991, the Ukrainian authorities did not have to restore the national identity because the state's history was always in the genes, traditions, and folklore.

chapters.title: Crimean Tatars are the Indigenous population of the Crimean Peninsula
chapters.text:
During his reign, Joseph Stalin committed many crimes, one of the most prominent examples being the deportation of 190,000 Crimean Tatars to various republics. About half died during or in the first years after deportation. After Ukraine's independence declaration, the Crimean Tatars returned to their lands, encountering discrimination and rejection. Negative narratives that they were dangerous and occupied the land illegally spread throughout society.

The Soviet rule erased references to the Indigenous people of Crimea from the pages of history textbooks because the latter refused to renounce their religion and traditions. Moreover, the Khan's Palace in Bakhchisarai became known as the architectural wonder of the Russian Empire. The government manipulated historical facts so that the “Soviet people” forgot about the existence of the Crimean Tatars and considered them enemies. As a result, Crimean Tatars couldn’t buy property or had to pay an inflated price in the early 1990s. Despite the hardships, they returned and united even more. Families supported and helped each other build temporary accommodations and, eventually, houses. They often received guests who had returned from other socialist republics and needed accommodation. The Crimean Tatars never felt part of the Soviet Union; on the contrary, the government was hostile, and the people experienced repression and deportations.

Before Catherine II annexed the peninsula in the 18th century, 95% of the Crimea population were Crimean Tatars. In 2013, the number was 14%.

The language issue has also always been acute. The Indigenous population spoke the Crimean Tatar language; the older generations insisted that young people learn and practice the language despite it being challenging. Consuming Russian-language content was much easier since it prevailed. However, the current situation is fundamentally different. Speaking in one's native language has become prestigious, even among young people. However, since the Russian occupation, only seven of the 15 Crimean Tatar schools have remained, and the Russian authorities are trying to ensure the Russian language prevails. They formed the concept of “Crimean people,” similar to the “Soviet,” to create a platform for Russian propaganda and shift the focus from distinctive ethnic groups.

Religion also plays an essential role. Crimean Tatars are Muslims, and the percentage of atheists is very low. The major holidays are Kurban Bayram and Oraza Bayram. They also have traditions of celebrating weddings and carrying out funeral rites. This nation lies between the Eastern Turkic world and the Western European world and understands both sides.

Regardless of their origin, Crimean Tatars consider themselves part of the Ukrainian nation because they share and respect the same values.

Today, the Crimean Tatars play a leading role in the development of the Ukrainian political nation, and are a bright and visible part of the current multicultural and multi-confessional identity of Ukraine. ~ Volodymyr Yermolenko

Volodymyr Yermolenko

Did you know? The Crimean Tatars have three sub-ethnic groups — Yali Boylu (southern coast), Nogai (steppe), and Tata (mountaineers from Bakhchisaray).

chapters.title: Memories of Donbas by Volodymyr Rafayenko
chapters.text:
In 2014, Russia started a war in Donbas, violating the territorial integrity of Ukraine and forcing many to leave their homes and move to different cities in Ukraine. The same happened to Volodymyr Rafayenko, a Ukrainian writer and novelist who wrote works in Russian from 1992 to 2018 and won several literary prizes in Russia. When armed militants entered Donetsk to “protect the Russian-speaking population,” he had to move from his hometown, where he lived for 45 years. Before the invasion, he wrote and spoke Russian and studied Russian literature professionally. He never felt discomfort doing so in Ukraine. Therefore, he was confused regarding whom the militants were protecting him from. Due to increased danger, Volodymyr Rafayenko started from scratch and found a new home for himself and his family elsewhere.

The Russian invasion was so shameful and gratuitous that the writer decided to learn Ukrainian and start writing in it. In the small towns and villages of Donbas, the Ukrainian language was widespread, while Russian prevailed in large cities. However, the Ukrainian language there changed under the influence of Russian; Rafayenko calls it “Eastern Ukrainian.” The Russian language in Donbas also differs from the one spoken in Russia. Russian spread there during the Soviet Union, especially in educational institutions and enterprises, artificially displacing Ukrainian. Therefore, the generation of Volodymyr Rafayenko's parents spoke Russian, but his grandparents were Ukrainian-speaking. Sometimes, they shared memories that were incomprehensible to him in his youth about the artificial famine (Holodomor in the 1930s), which the Soviet authorities arranged in Ukraine.

Russia often states traditional Russian clothes, like kokoshniks and sundresses, were worn in Luhansk and Donetsk regions. However, the main headdresses in the Donbas were caps, flower crowns, and scarves; they also wore vyshyvankas with unique ornaments.

The war, which began in 2014, finally erased the colonial myth of “fraternal nations” and raised the civic identity question acutely. Violent attempts to convert the residents of Donbas to their ideology met resistance and caused the opposite reaction that the Russians expected. Like many other citizens, Volodymyr Rafayenko will forever remember the brutal invasion by the country whose culture he studied for years. He recalls the “Donetsk-Kyiv” night train, which split his life into “before” and “after.”

For a long time, the Soviet authorities isolated Donbas as a region to control the industry. Later, the Russians used it in propaganda during the war. However, such separation is artificial and groundless: Ukraine and everything Ukrainian has always been nearer and dearer to residents there than Russian. The region's residents have never considered themselves a part of the Russian Federation. Still, during the 70 years of the Soviet Union, Donbas formed a cultural separation, with Ukrainian culture at its core. Examples are evident in everyday life: children read Ukrainian fairy tales, people sing Ukrainian songs, and Ukrainian traditions are continued on holidays, particularly Christmas.

chapters.title: Communication is vital for Ukraine's security
chapters.text:
We could talk endlessly about Ukraine, but dry facts are enough: Ukraine is the second largest country in Europe, spreading more than a thousand kilometers from east to west — with the geographical center of Europe on its western border — and 46 million citizens.

Surprisingly, this country was considered a buffer zone from the 1990s to the 2000s — a place the international community could not identify. Russia propelled such narratives since it always benefited from Ukraine’s buffer zone image. The West didn’t see it as an economic partner because Ukraine would never be a stable and safe environment. Nowadays, an even more ambiguous concept has replaced this perception with “in-betweenness,” which implies the absence of Ukraine's subjectivity and dismisses Ukraine as an object of foreign policy of neighboring states. Such myths are dangerous because they spread quickly and remain in the informative space.

Another narrative Russia imposed on Ukraine and the whole world from the war’s beginning is, “You can't fight Russia.” But the first days of the full-scale invasion demonstrated these are empty intimidation tactics. No matter how aggressive and emotional the struggle is, the most precious things are at stake — freedom and future security. In some places, this narrative still appears in international media and organizations because they are outside the situation and do not want to lose valuable connections. But Ukraine proves every day that standing up to Russia is possible. Ukrainian society has already paid with the lives of almost 13,000 people (according to the UN), of which one-third are civilians, and 30,000 are wounded. Despite the terrible statistics, it continues to resist.

A frozen conflict is the worst scenario for Ukraine because it delays the solution. During the war, Ukrainians learned to fight on the military and diplomatic fronts. But sometimes, there’s more to discuss than the struggle, so the freezing of conflicts arises. In this context, a “catch-22” occurs; people are ready, but governments cannot agree. After a while, governments understand the need for communication, but the population gets used to living with conflict. A whole generation may grow up seeing the adversary as pure evil. Then, the leaders are unsure whether their people will accept the peace they desire. Federalization, which involves the formation of separate states from annexed territories, is also an unsuitable scenario for Ukraine, which has suffered from the distribution of its lands between different countries for centuries.

Developing resilience and partnerships instead of buffer zones can help overcome a country's vulnerable security conditions.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Ukraine, its past, and present remain a mystery to many. After Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine on February 24, 2022, the news spread worldwide and remained on the front pages of the media for several months. However, talking about Ukraine is still critical in the historical and cultural time frame and today. Ukraine continues to develop and achieve a lot despite the setbacks. It is one of the largest countries in Europe, dating back to the founding of Kyivan Rus in the 9th century. Thanks to its fertile black soil, Ukraine is a notable exporter of oil, grains, sugar, vegetables, and fruits.

These fertile lands allowed the first tribes to settle on the state's territory, where they constantly fought with nomads. When the latter won, they stayed in these territories, adopting the culture and customs of their predecessors. Thus, the founders of Rus were the Vikings who came from the north and later accepted Christianity and baptized the pagans.

The creation of the Ukrainian state is associated with the Cossacks. At the same time, the word “Ukraine” defined statehood. Popularization of the Cossack myth took place in literature thanks to many artists, including Taras Shevchenko.

Due to the USSR’s influence, the myth that Ukrainians and Russians are one people prevailed for centuries. However, after analyzing the history and cultural features, it becomes an evident lie that authorities skillfully used for propaganda. The implanted image of the “Soviet man” denied and erased the ethnic characteristics of the peoples of many contemporary republics. Notably, such fate met the Crimean Tatars, whom Stalin deported from Crimea, their homeland. Due to the Russian occupation, the Tatars still face oppression by the Russian authorities.

The Russian Federation continues to popularize its propaganda today. In particular, in 2014, Russian militants violated the territorial integrity of Ukraine and occupied Donbas with the fictitious purpose of protecting the Russian-speaking population. However, these events and the full-scale war united Ukrainians more and turned them away from their Eastern neighbor. Ukrainian resilience, well-established communication with the world, and moral values worth fighting for help its people resist the violent invaders daily.
